nodes.title: Gender & articles: le, la, un, une

explanation.header: Learn the article as part of the word
explanation.body: French nouns never appear naked — every noun carries le or la, and the article is where French stores gender. Learn le café and la rue as single units and gender never becomes a separate memorization project; it rides along for free.
Four words cover almost everything, and both definite articles collapse to l' before a vowel — that's why you hear l'eau and l'hôtel.
bullets: le / la — the (l' before a vowel: l'eau, l'école)
un / une — a
les — the, plural, both genders; des is the plural of un/une

explanation.body: Gender looks arbitrary, but a word's ending predicts it with high reliability. These two rules alone cover thousands of words — including every -tion cognate you got for free: situation, solution, réservation are all la.
bullets: -tion, -sion, -té → la (la situation, la liberté)
-age, -ment, -eau → le (le message, le moment, le gâteau)
exceptions exist (le problème, la plage) — collect them as they come, don't study them

explanation.body: A wrong article almost never breaks understanding — un grande café still gets you a large coffee. French children get gender wrong for years and nobody stops them mid-sentence. Get it right where the ending tells you, shrug where it doesn't, and keep talking.

Fast French drops ne and swallows syllables, but the article survives everything — it is glued to the noun at any speed. That makes it a parsing cue: when speech blurs, the le or la you catch tells you a noun just went by.

canonical_examples.formal: Est-ce que tu veux une table ?

canonical_examples.english: Did you see the girl who speaks French?
canonical_examples.formal: Tu as vu la fille qui parle français ?
canonical_examples.street: T'as vu la fille qui parle français ?

nodes.title: Plurals & adjective agreement

explanation.body: Written French adds -s to nouns and adjectives, but that -s is silent. The word doing the work in speech is the article: le becomes les, un becomes des. Train your ear on that switch — the noun itself sounds identical in singular and plural.

examples.english: I want some friends. (amis sounds like ami)

explanation.body: Des is the plural of un/une. English often drops 'some' entirely, but French keeps des — leave it out and the sentence sounds clipped and odd.

examples.french: On va manger des petites choses.
examples.english: We're going to eat some small things.

explanation.body: Adjectives match the gender and number of their noun: petit, petite, petits, petites. In most cases this changes only the spelling, not the sound. The plural -s on petits is as silent as the one on amis.

Some adjectives end in a consonant that is silent in the masculine but pronounced in the feminine — because adding -e wakes the consonant up. This is the one case where agreement actually changes what you hear.
bullets: petit → petite (the t sounds in the feminine)
bon → bonne (the n sounds; the vowel shifts too)
blanc → blanche (the c becomes /ʃ/)

examples.french: C'est pas pareil, la table blanche et les tables blanches.
examples.english: It's not the same, the white table and the white tables.

explanation.header: On the street: hear le/les first
explanation.body: Fast spoken French compresses everything, but the article survives. Catching le versus les — or un versus des — is your fastest signal that a singular or plural noun just passed. Noun endings won't help you; the article always will.

nodes.title: Adjective placement: before or after?

explanation.body: In French, adjectives almost always follow the noun. This is the reverse of English, and it's simply where French settled historically. When you don't know where an adjective goes, put it after — you'll be right most of the time.

examples.french: Un café noir. / T'as une question difficile ?
examples.english: A black coffee. / Do you have a difficult question?

explanation.body: A small group of the most common adjectives in the language always go before the noun. Because you use them constantly, they quickly become automatic.
bullets: bon / bonne — good
petit / petite — small
grand / grande — big, great
gros / grosse — big, fat
beau / belle — beautiful
jeune — young
vieux / vieille — old
nouveau / nouvelle — new
mauvais / mauvaise — bad

explanation.body: Seeing them in place is more useful than seeing them listed. Notice the adjective sits between the article and the noun every time.

examples.french: C'est une bonne idée. / J'veux un petit café.
examples.english: It's a good idea. / I want a small coffee.

explanation.body: A few before-club adjectives change meaning depending on side. Un grand homme means a great man; un homme grand means a tall man. This happens with a handful of words and is worth knowing, but it's a detail — position and meaning together come clear from context.

In spoken French, before-club adjectives — especially gros, petit, and vrai — get used heavily to punch up a noun, the way English speakers load up 'total' or 'real'. They're not describing size so much as degree.

examples.french: C'est un gros problème. / C'est une vraie galère, chais pas.
examples.english: It's a serious problem. / It's a real nightmare, I don't know.

nodes.title: Prepositions: à, de, en, au, chez

explanation.body: à and de are the busiest prepositions in French. à marks where you're going or where you are; de marks where something comes from or who it belongs to. Everything else in this lesson builds on that pair.

examples.english: It's the dish of the day. (belonging)

explanation.header: Mandatory contractions with le and les
explanation.body: à and de cannot sit next to le or les — French fuses them every time, no exceptions. The contractions are phonetic: saying à le out loud is awkward in French the same way saying 'to the the café' would be in English.
bullets: à + le → au (au café, au bar)
à + les → aux (aux toilettes)
de + le → du (le plat du jour, le menu du restaurant)
de + les → des (la fin des vacances)
à la and de la stay as they are — only le and les trigger the fusion

explanation.body: Countries have grammatical gender; cities don't. Use en before feminine countries (most of them) and à before cities. When the country is masculine, use au instead of en.

examples.french: Elle est en France, à Paris.
Il vit au Japon, à Tokyo.
examples.english: He lives in Japan, in Tokyo.

explanation.body: chez covers 'at someone's place' or 'at a professional's practice' — anywhere defined by the person rather than a building name. It always takes a person or pronoun, never a thing.

examples.french: On doit aller chez le médecin.
examples.english: We have to go to the doctor's.

explanation.body: Prepositions don't drop in spoken French — au, du, chez stay intact at any speed. What changes is everything around them: je becomes j', nous becomes on, tu becomes t'. The preposition is the stable anchor in a fast sentence.

nodes.title: Question forms: the full toolkit

explanation.body: French has three question registers and you need all three — one to produce naturally, one to understand in formal contexts, and one to recognise in writing.
bullets: Intonation only — spoken default, no word order change
Est-ce que — neutral, works everywhere, nothing inverted
Inversion (viens-tu ?) — formal/written; recognise it, don't produce it

explanation.body: In spoken French, a rising voice at the end turns any statement into a question. Word order stays exactly the same as a statement — French doesn't rearrange the sentence the way English does.

Drop est-ce que before a normal statement and you have a question that works in any situation — not formal, not slangy. It's the register you use with your boss, with strangers, or when you want to sound clear.
These seven words pair with either est-ce que or the street pattern below.
bullets: quoi — what
où — where
quand — when
qui — who
comment — how
pourquoi — why
combien — how much / how many

explanation.header: Street French: word at the end
explanation.body: Casual spoken French moves the question word to the end of the sentence instead of the front. The sentence stays in statement order; the question word tags on after. This is the pattern you'll hear constantly — it's not lazy speech, it's the actual spoken norm.

Viens-tu ? and Où allez-vous ? are grammatically correct but belong to formal writing, official speeches, and teachers demonstrating grammar. No one expects you to produce inversion in conversation — your job is to hear it and understand it without freezing.

examples.english: Both mean 'Are you coming?' — only the register differs.

nodes.title: Beyond pas: jamais, rien, plus, personne

explanation.body: French negation has two parts: ne before the verb and a negative word after it. Pas is just the most common option in that second slot. Swap it out for jamais, rien, plus, or personne and the structure stays exactly the same.
Four words, four meanings — none of them require a double negative in English even though French keeps two negative markers.
bullets: jamais — never: il vient jamais
rien — nothing / not anything: j'ai rien dit
plus — no more / not anymore: elle veut plus rien
personne — nobody / not anyone: y'a jamais personne ici

explanation.body: In spoken French, ne disappears with every one of these words, not just with pas. This is the rule in conversation, not a shortcut. If you keep the ne, you sound like a formal document.

examples.french: J'vais jamais là-bas. / Il vient jamais. / J'ai rien dit.
examples.english: I never go there. / He never comes. / I didn't say anything.

explanation.body: When plus means 'no more', its s is silent — j'en peux plus sounds like 'plu'. This distinguishes it from plus meaning 'more' (e.g. plus vite, where the s is heard). Context usually makes it obvious, but the pronunciation is the reliable signal.
Rien and personne can move to the front of the sentence as subjects. When they do, ne stays glued to the verb in writing, but drops in speech the same as anywhere else.
French can stack jamais with personne or rien in one sentence — something English can't do grammatically. Both words sit in the same post-verb zone, and ne (or its absence) covers the whole thing.

examples.french: Y'a jamais personne ici. / Elle veut plus rien.
examples.english: There's never anyone here. / She doesn't want anything anymore.

nodes.title: Object pronoun order: me le, te la, le lui

explanation.body: When a verb takes both a direct and an indirect object pronoun, they stack in a fixed sequence. The sequence depends on which pronouns are involved — there are two different rules, not one.
bullets: me / te / nous / vous come BEFORE le / la / les — tu me le donnes
le / la / les come BEFORE lui / leur — je le lui dis

explanation.body: French keeps person pronouns (me, te, nous, vous) close to the verb because they refer to people already in the conversation. But lui and leur are third-person and behave differently — they yield to le/la/les. The split feels arbitrary until you notice the pattern: first-person and second-person pronouns win the front slot; lui/leur always sit behind the direct object.

explanation.header: The whole cluster sits before the verb
explanation.body: Both pronouns land together, immediately before the conjugated verb — or before the infinitive if there are two verbs. In a compound tense like passé composé, they sit before the auxiliary.

examples.english: Can you give it to me?
He told me. (me + le contracts to m'l' before the auxiliary a)

explanation.body: When le or la precedes a verb starting with a vowel — including the auxiliary a or ai — it contracts to l'. This is the same elision rule as with articles; the pronoun behaves identically.

examples.english: He told me. (me le a → me l'a)
I told you. (te le ai → te l'ai)

explanation.body: At normal spoken speed, the pronoun cluster collapses — vowels drop, consonants merge. Your immediate goal is recognition; producing the compressed forms will come with exposure.

examples.english: He's giving it to me. (il me le → il m'le)
I told you. (je te l'ai → j't'l'ai — three words crushed into one pulse)

nodes.title: y & en: the two little pronouns, deep dive

explanation.body: French hates repeating a noun phrase that the listener already tracks. Y and en are the two pronouns that handle that job for specific grammatical slots: y covers à + thing or place, and en covers de + thing plus anything involving a quantity.

examples.french: Tu penses à ce problème ? — J'y pense.
examples.english: Are you thinking about that problem? — I'm thinking about it.

examples.french: Tu veux du café ? — J'en veux.
examples.english: Do you want some coffee? — I want some.

explanation.header: Y: replaces à + thing or place
explanation.body: When the verb takes à — penser à, aller à, croire à — y replaces the whole à + noun chunk. Place works the same way: à Paris, au bureau, là-bas all become y. The pronoun sits directly before the verb.

explanation.header: En: replaces de + thing and quantities
explanation.body: En replaces de + noun (parler de, avoir besoin de, venir de) and, critically, it carries any number or quantity word along with it — the number follows the verb rather than disappearing.
bullets: de + noun → en: Tu parles de ça ? J'en parle.
number/quantity stays after verb: J'en veux deux. J'en ai plein.

examples.french: Il y en a trois. → Y'en a trois.

explanation.body: Several of the most common spoken chunks are frozen around y or en. Don't analyze them — absorb them as single units, because their internal logic isn't always transparent from the modern language.
bullets: Ça y est ! — That's it, done, finally.
On y va ! — Let's go.
J'en peux plus. — I can't take it anymore.
T'en fais pas. — Don't worry about it.

examples.french: J'en peux plus. → J'en peux plus.

explanation.body: Il y a shrinks to y'a in everyday speech, and il y en a shrinks to y'en a. These two forms are the spoken existentials — you'll hear them dozens of times a day to say what exists, what's left, what's available. The negative y'en a plus (nothing left) is equally constant.

examples.french: Y'en a plus. (Il n'y en a plus.)
Y'en a trois. (Il y en a trois.)

nodes.title: Comparatives & superlatives: plus, moins, aussi

explanation.body: French comparatives work the same way in any direction: drop plus, moins, or aussi in front of the adjective, then use que to introduce what you're comparing to. The adjective still agrees in gender and number as normal.

examples.english: It's more expensive than in Paris.

explanation.body: Bon and bien each have their own comparative — you can't use plus with them. Meilleur replaces plus bon; mieux replaces plus bien. Meilleur is an adjective so it agrees; mieux never changes.
bullets: bon (adjective) → meilleur/meilleure: Ce resto est meilleur que l'autre.
bien (adverb) → mieux: C'est mieux ici.

explanation.body: Add le, la, or les before plus or moins and you get the superlative. The article matches the noun it belongs to, and the adjective keeps its normal position relative to that noun.

examples.french: C'est carrément la solution la plus simple.

explanation.body: French has two unrelated words spelled plus, and they sound different. When plus means 'more' (comparative or superlative), the s is always pronounced. When plus means 'no more' or 'not any more' in a negative, the s is silent. Same sentence, different meanings — context separates them.

examples.french: J'en veux plus — c'est plus intéressant là-bas.
examples.english: I don't want any more — it's more interesting over there.

explanation.body: Spoken French leans heavily on c'est as the carrier for comparisons — it's shorter and more natural than constructing a full subject-verb phrase. Carrément (absolutely, totally) regularly intensifies superlatives in casual speech.
bullets: c'est mieux — preferred over il est mieux in most spoken contexts
c'est moins cher — the go-to for price comparisons
c'est carrément plus simple — carrément pushes emphasis without raising your voice

nodes.title: Imperatives: commands without a subject

explanation.body: A command has no subject because the person you're talking to is obvious. French simply removes it. The verb is all you need.

explanation.header: -er verbs lose the s in tu-form
explanation.body: In normal present tense, tu manges has an s. In the imperative that s disappears: mange ! This applies to every -er verb, including aller. The reason is historical and there's no logic to recover — just know that if it ends in -er, the tu command ends in a bare -e.

examples.english: Eat! / Go on then! (aller keeps the s only before y)

explanation.body: In a positive command, object pronouns move after the verb and connect with a hyphen. Me becomes moi in that position.

Wrap the verb in ne … pas to negate a command. In spoken French, ne drops constantly — you'll hear touche pas ça far more than ne touche pas ça. Both are correct; knowing both lets you understand and choose.

examples.french: Ne touche pas ça ! → Touche pas ça !

examples.english: Don't worry. (street: the pas drops too — the whole phrase shrinks to t'inquiète)

explanation.body: A bare imperative is direct but not rude — context does a lot. Adding s'il te plaît (informal) or s'il vous plaît (formal/plural) softens it where needed.

examples.french: Vas-y, s'il te plaît ! / Allez-y, s'il vous plaît !

explanation.body: Real spoken French runs on a short list of imperatives. These turn up in almost every conversation — recognising them as commands is half the battle.
bullets: Vas-y — go ahead / come on / go for it
Attends — wait (also: hold on a sec)
Arrête — stop it
Laisse tomber — forget it / drop it
T'inquiète — don't worry (ne and pas both gone)

drills.french_formal: Ne touche pas ça, s'il te plaît.
drills.french_street: Touche pas ça, s'il te plaît.

drills.french_formal: Ne t'inquiète pas, ce n'est pas grave.

drills.english: Eat something, you want to eat, right?
drills.french_formal: Mange quelque chose, tu veux manger, non ?
drills.french_street: Mange quelque chose, tu veux manger, non ?

drills.french_formal: Regarde ça, ce n'est pas possible !

drills.french_formal: Ne va pas au restaurant sans moi.
drills.french_street: Va pas au resto sans moi.

Let it go, it's not serious.

nodes.title: Reflexive verbs: the daily-routine engine

explanation.body: A reflexive verb means the subject acts on itself — or, with two people, on each other. French makes this visible with a small pronoun that sits between the subject and the verb. Remove that pronoun and you lose the meaning entirely: lever means to raise; se lever means to get (oneself) up.
The pronoun matches the subject, always placed directly before the verb.
bullets: je me lève — I get up
tu te lèves — you get up
il/elle/on se lève — he/she/one gets up
nous nous levons / vous vous levez — we/you get up

explanation.body: Daily-routine verbs and social verbs between them cover most of what you'll actually say.
bullets: Routine: se lever, se réveiller, se doucher, s'habiller, se coucher
Social: se voir, s'appeler, se retrouver, se parler

examples.french: On va se retrouver au café.
examples.english: We're going to meet up at the café.

explanation.body: With on, nous, or plural subjects, the same construction expresses 'each other'. On se voit doesn't mean everyone looks at themselves — it means the people involved see each other. Context makes it clear.

examples.french: On s'est vus hier — ça faisait longtemps.
examples.english: We saw each other yesterday — it had been a long time.

explanation.body: Reflexive verbs use être, not avoir, in the passé composé — so the past participle agrees with the subject. Add -e for feminine, -s for plural. The pronoun stays before être, not before the participle.

examples.french: Je me suis levé tard ce matin.
examples.english: I got up late this morning. (speaker is male)
We saw each other yesterday. (-s because the subject is plural)

explanation.body: In spoken French, je me contracts almost universally to j'me — and in fast speech that second e drops too, giving j'm'. This is not slang; it is simply how the mouth works at normal speed. The same pressure applies to te when it follows a vowel.

nodes.title: Partitives: du, de la, des — 'some'

explanation.body: In English you can say 'I want bread' and skip 'some'. French cannot. Every time a noun refers to an unspecified quantity of something, the partitive article is obligatory — drop it and the sentence sounds broken.

examples.french: J'veux du pain. / Y'a du café et des frites.
examples.english: I want (some) bread. / There's (some) coffee and (some) fries.

explanation.body: The partitive is built from de + the definite article, so it follows gender and number exactly like le/la/les does.
bullets: du (= de + le) — masculine singular: du pain, du café
de la — feminine singular: de la bière
de l' — before a vowel sound, either gender: de l'eau, de l'argent
des — plural: des frites, des trucs

explanation.body: After a negative, all four partitive forms reduce to plain de (or d' before a vowel). The logic: negation cancels the quantity entirely, so the article that marks quantity disappears too.

examples.french: J'ai pas de pain. / Y'a plus d'lait.
examples.english: I don't have any bread. / There's no more milk.

explanation.body: Any expression that states a measure — beaucoup de, un peu de, trop de, assez de — follows the same rule as negation: the article drops and you get bare de. The quantity word is already doing the job the article would do.

examples.french: Y'a beaucoup d'monde et trop d'trucs à faire.
examples.english: There are a lot of people and too many things to do.

explanation.body: Ordering food or drink is where partitives come up most. Je prends and je voudrais both take a partitive directly: je prends du café, je voudrais de l'eau. Use these two frames to anchor the whole system in something practical.

examples.french: On va prendre d'la bière au café — on a pas beaucoup d'temps.
examples.english: We're going to have some beer at the café — we haven't got much time.

explanation.header: Street French: partitives survive, but compress
explanation.body: Fast speech never drops the partitive — that would be a grammar error, not a style choice. What it does do is glue de to the noun when a vowel follows: d'lait, d'eau, d'monde. The article is still there; it's just swallowed into the noun at speed.

canonical_examples.formal: Je voudrais de l'eau, s'il vous plaît.

nodes.title: il faut & impersonal expressions

explanation.body: Il faut doesn't refer to any person — il is a dummy subject, like 'it' in 'it's raining'. That's why the same form works for everyone: you, me, all of us. No conjugation to choose, no subject to match.

examples.english: You have to leave. / We have to leave.

explanation.body: Faut takes either an infinitive (action) or a noun (thing needed). The meaning shifts naturally.
bullets: il faut + infinitive → obligation: il faut voir ça (you have to see that)
il faut + noun → necessity: il faut une réservation (we need a reservation), il faut du temps (it takes time)

explanation.header: Faut vs. tu dois for advice
explanation.body: Tu dois is direct and slightly heavy — it points a finger. Faut is impersonal, so it lands softer, more like general wisdom than a personal accusation. That's why native speakers default to faut when giving advice.

examples.english: You shouldn't do that. (gentler than tu ne dois pas)

explanation.body: Faut isn't alone — several impersonal expressions work the same way: no subject to choose, fixed form, drop straight into a sentence.
bullets: il vaut mieux + infinitive — il vaut mieux attendre (it's better to wait)
ça prend — ça prend du temps (it takes time)
il reste — il reste du pain (there's some bread left)

explanation.body: To push faut into the past, swap it for fallait — nothing else changes. It covers 'should have' and 'had to'.
In real spoken French, the il disappears entirely — faut alone carries the sentence. This isn't sloppy; it's the norm. Vaut mieux loses its il the same way. Keep the il in writing; lose it in speech.
bullets: il faut partir → faut partir
il faut voir ça → faut voir ça
il ne faut pas → faut pas (ne also drops)
il vaut mieux attendre → vaut mieux attendre

canonical_examples.formal: Il ne faut pas faire ça.

nodes.title: Time & date expressions

explanation.body: The frame is always il est + number + heures. Minutes after the hour are added directly; two special phrases handle the quarters.
bullets: et quart — quarter past (il est neuf heures et quart)
et demie — half past (il est trois heures et demie)
moins le quart — quarter to the next hour (il est huit heures moins le quart)

examples.french: Il est trois heures et demie.
Il est huit heures moins le quart — j'dois partir.
examples.english: It's quarter to eight — I have to leave.

explanation.body: Written timetables, official announcements, and workplace calendars all run on the 24-hour clock. You don't need to produce it in casual speech, but you'll see and hear it constantly, so learn to read it fast: just subtract 12 for any hour above 12.

examples.french: La réunion est à quatorze heures trente.
examples.english: The meeting is at two thirty p.m.

explanation.header: Days and months are lowercase, article-free
explanation.body: Unlike English, French never capitalises days or months. More usefully: you never need un or le in front of them when stating a day or month alone — lundi means 'on Monday' or 'Monday', and en janvier means 'in January'. Adding le lundi shifts the meaning to 'every Monday'.
bullets: Days: lundi, mardi, mercredi, jeudi, vendredi, samedi, dimanche
Months: janvier, février, mars, avril, mai, juin… — all lowercase
en + month: en mars, en octobre

explanation.body: These words sit in front of any verb or standalone as complete chunks — learn them as a set because they cover almost every temporal reference in real conversation.
bullets: aujourd'hui — today
demain — tomorrow
hier — yesterday
ce matin / ce soir — this morning / this evening
la semaine prochaine — next week

explanation.header: à pins a time to an event
explanation.body: Use à before any clock time or time-anchor word to mean 'at' for appointments and departures. French compresses this into chunks — à demain is a full sentence meaning 'see you tomorrow', because the à already implies 'until' or 'at our next meeting'.

explanation.header: depuis for duration that's still running
explanation.body: English says 'I have lived here for two years' — past tense, because it started in the past. French sees the situation as present and uses depuis with the present tense. If you use the past tense with depuis, French speakers will understand you've stopped doing the thing.

examples.english: I've been living here for two years (and still do).

explanation.body: Schedule talk compresses heavily in spoken French. À toute (short for à toute à l'heure) means 'see you soon'; c'est quand ? ('when is it?') is the default for asking about any event. These chunks are not sloppy speech — they are the normal register for any time-related exchange.
bullets: à toute — see you soon (drops à l'heure entirely)
à demain / à lundi — see you tomorrow / Monday
c'est quand ? — when is it?

nodes.title: Pointing & owning: ce, cette, mon, ton, son

explanation.header: Ce / cette / ces: this and that
explanation.body: French uses the same word for both 'this' and 'that' — context handles the distance. The form changes only to match the noun's gender and number.
bullets: ce + masculine singular: ce mec, ce truc
cette + feminine singular: cette table, cette meuf
ces + any plural: ces gens, ces trucs
cet + masculine singular starting with a vowel or silent h: cet après-midi, cet homme — the extra t keeps vowels from crashing

explanation.body: When you want to stress 'that one right there' — or contrast two things — tack -là onto the noun. It costs nothing and sounds natural at any register.

examples.english: I want that thing right there.

examples.french: Cette table-là — ton café, il est dessus.
examples.english: That table there — your coffee is on it.

explanation.header: Possessives track the noun, not the owner
explanation.body: This is the one rule that trips almost everyone: mon/ton/son agrees with the thing owned, not with who owns it. Sa voiture means 'his car' or 'her car' — sa is feminine because voiture is feminine, full stop.

examples.english: His / her car — that's impossible. (sa = feminine because voiture is feminine)

explanation.header: The three sets you use most
explanation.body: Singular forms split by gender; plural doesn't bother.
bullets: mon / ma / mes — my
ton / ta / tes — your (informal)
son / sa / ses — his, her, its

explanation.header: Mon before any feminine vowel noun
explanation.body: Ma becomes mon before a feminine noun starting with a vowel — spoken French refuses the vowel collision. The noun is still feminine; only the possessive shifts form.

examples.french: Mon amie — elle lui a parlé.
examples.english: My friend — she spoke to him. (amie is feminine; mon replaces ma to avoid the clash)

explanation.body: Demonstratives are how spoken French points and comments — ce mec, cette meuf, ce truc-là are all over casual speech. Notre / votre / leur work the same way as the others but come up less in conversation; pick them up as you hear them.

examples.english: These people, I don't know them.

nodes.title: c'est vs il est

explanation.body: French splits 'to be' between c'est and il/elle est, and each one has a clear lane. C'est points at something — identifying it, naming it, labelling it. Il/elle est describes something already in focus — adding a quality to a person or thing you've already named.

Whenever you're answering 'what is this?' or 'who is that?', use c'est. It introduces a noun, a name, or a stressed pronoun. The thing you're pointing at hasn't been named yet — c'est does the naming.
bullets: c'est + noun: C'est un café, c'est la solution
c'est + name: C'est Marie, c'est Paul
c'est + stressed pronoun: C'est moi, c'est toi, c'est lui

explanation.header: Il/elle est for adjectives after naming
explanation.body: Once a person or thing is already on the table, use il est or elle est to attach a bare adjective to them. The adjective agrees with the gender of what was named — that's why it's elle est sympa for a woman and il est sympa for a man.

examples.french: Tu connais Paul ? Il est sympa.
examples.english: Do you know Paul? He's nice.

examples.french: La fille qui parle — elle est française.
examples.english: The girl who's talking — she's French.

explanation.body: When you're commenting on a situation rather than describing a named person or thing, use c'est — even with an adjective. Here c'est doesn't refer to any specific he or she; it points at the whole situation. This is why c'est difficile and c'est bon don't use il.

explanation.header: Professions: the article flips the frame
explanation.body: With professions, both frames are possible but they mean subtly different things. Il/elle est + bare profession treats the job like an adjective — a category, not a full noun phrase. Once you add an article (un, une), you've made it a noun phrase, so c'est takes over. The article is the signal.
bullets: bare profession → elle est médecin, il est prof
article + profession → c'est un médecin, c'est une prof

explanation.body: If you're unsure which to use, c'est is almost always safe in spoken French. Native speakers lean on it heavily — il est is more common in writing and formal speech. Getting the distinction right matters most for professions and for bare adjectives after a named person.

explanation.header: C'est carrying more load on the street
explanation.body: In casual speech, c'est takes on extra work that written French would handle differently. It anchors questions, reactions, and verdicts. C'est pas is the standard everyday negation — ne drops completely.
bullets: identity question: c'est qui ? (who is it?)
what-is-this: c'est quoi ça ? (what's that?)
verdict: c'est mort (it's dead / no chance)
negation: c'est pas possible, c'est pas grave

canonical_examples.formal: C'est une médecin. / Elle est médecin.
canonical_examples.street: C'est une médecin. / Elle est médecin.

canonical_examples.formal: C'est difficile, je ne sais pas.

nodes.title: Just did / doing right now: venir de & être en train de

explanation.body: French uses venir de + infinitive for 'just did' and être en train de + infinitive for 'doing right now'. In both cases only the first verb conjugates — venir or être changes for person and tense, the infinitive after de stays exactly as it appears in a dictionary.

examples.french: Je viens de manger. / Je suis en train de manger.
examples.english: I just ate. / I'm in the middle of eating.

explanation.body: English encodes recency and ongoing action inside the tense ('I just ate', 'I'm eating'). French keeps its tense system separate and uses these wrapper frames instead. The frame carries all the meaning; you only need to drop your verb into it as an infinitive.

examples.french: On est en train de travailler.
examples.english: We're in the middle of working.

explanation.body: Put venir or être into the imparfait and you get the past equivalents. These work exactly as they do in imparfait storytelling you already know — background states and ongoing situations.
bullets: je venais de partir — I had just left
j'étais en train de manger — I was in the middle of eating

examples.french: Il venait de partir quand t'as appelé.
examples.english: He had just left when you called.

explanation.body: You now have three frames that map the full timeline around any moment. Combine them and a French speaker can pinpoint exactly where an action sits.
bullets: je viens de manger — just did (past edge)
je suis en train de manger — doing right now
je vais manger — about to (futur proche)

explanation.body: Fast speech squeezes the frame but never removes it — the meaning would be lost without it. Je becomes j', tu es becomes chuis or j'suis, and il vient de contracts the de before a consonant-starting infinitive.

nodes.title: Futur simple: when it beats futur proche

explanation.body: Take the infinitive and glue on -ai, -as, -a, -ez, -ont. That's the whole rule for regular verbs. For -re verbs, drop the final e first: prendre → prendr-, then add the endings.
bullets: parler → parlerai, parleras, parlera, parlerez, parleront
finir → finirai, finiras, finira, finirez, finiront
prendre → prendrai, prendras, prendra, prendrez, prendront

explanation.body: The stem changes, but the endings stay exactly the same. These eight cover almost every irregular you'll meet in real speech.
bullets: être → ser- (je serai)
avoir → aur- (t'auras)
aller → ir- (j'irai)
faire → fer- (on fera)
pouvoir → pourr- (tu pourras)
vouloir → voudr- (il voudra)
falloir → faudr- (il faudra)
venir → viendr- (tu viendras)

explanation.body: Futur proche is the spoken default for plans; futur simple adds distance or weight. It's the natural tense for promises and formal commitments, for events that feel remote or uncertain, and it's required after quand when the when-clause is in the future.

examples.french: Quand tu seras là, on commencera.

explanation.body: In English, 'when you arrive' uses a present tense. In French, if the action is in the future, both the quand-clause and the main clause must use futur simple — the present tense is not allowed there. This is one rule worth knowing explicitly.

examples.french: Quand il aura l'argent, il partira.
examples.english: When he has the money, he'll leave.

explanation.body: Spoken French leans hard on futur proche for everyday plans. But futur simple survives in fixed phrases that have been worn smooth by use — these you'll hear constantly and should lock in whole.
bullets: on verra — we'll see (dismissive or genuine)
ça ira — it'll be fine
tu verras — you'll see
j'te dirai — I'll let you know / I'll tell you

nodes.title: Survival subjunctive: il faut que + the forms you'll hear

explanation.body: After certain phrases — most importantly il faut que ('I/you/we have to') — French switches the next verb into a second set of forms called the subjunctive. Textbooks make this a mountain. In speech it is a toll booth: a handful of trigger phrases, and a handful of verbs whose subjunctive you will actually hear.

The subjunctive marks that an action is wanted, required, or doubted rather than stated as fact. Je sais qu'il vient — fact, normal form. Il faut qu'il vienne — requirement, subjunctive. You are not describing the world; you are pushing on it. That is the entire concept.

examples.french: Je sais qu'il vient. / Il faut qu'il vienne.
examples.english: I know he's coming. / He has to come.

explanation.body: For most verbs the subjunctive sounds identical to the present you already know (il faut que tu manges). Only a few very common verbs have audibly different forms — own these eight and you are covered:
bullets: être → sois / soit — faut que tu sois là
avoir → aies / ait — faut qu'il ait le temps
aller → aille — faut que j'aille
faire → fasse — faut qu'on fasse ça
pouvoir → puisse — pour que tu puisses venir
savoir → sache — faut que tu saches
venir → vienne — faut qu'elle vienne
prendre → prenne — faut que je prenne le train

explanation.header: One trigger does most of the work
explanation.body: Il faut que + subjunctive is the spoken workhorse for obligation — in conversation it largely replaces devoir. Learn it as a frame you drop verbs into, not as grammar to assemble.

examples.english: I've got to go. (the #1 way to leave anywhere)

explanation.body: The il drops — faut que j'aille, faut pas que t'oublies — and que elides to qu' before a vowel. If you keep one sentence from this lesson, keep faut que j'y aille; you will use it every day you are in France.

nodes.title: Si clauses: real ifs and imagined ifs

explanation.body: Si can mean two different things depending on tense. Use présent after si to say something real or likely; use imparfait to imagine something that isn't true right now. The tense after si is the signal — the result clause follows automatically.

examples.french: Si t'as le temps, on y va.
examples.english: If you have time, we'll go.

examples.french: Si j'avais le temps, je viendrais.
examples.english: If I had time, I'd come.

explanation.body: Futur and conditionnel never follow si directly — ever. Si + futur and si + conditionnel are simply not French sentences. The conditionnel lives in the result clause, not in the si clause.
bullets: ✗ Si je viendrai… — impossible
✗ Si j'aurais le temps… — impossible
✓ Si je viens… / Si j'avais le temps, je viendrais.

explanation.body: When si on is followed by the imparfait with a question intonation, it becomes a soft suggestion — the French equivalent of 'how about we…'. There's no separate result clause; the si clause does all the work.

examples.english: How about we go to the café?

explanation.body: Si shortens to s' before il and ils because French avoids two vowels colliding. Before elle, elle, or any other word starting with a vowel, si stays si — no elision.
bullets: s'il te plaît — before il ✓
s'il pleut — before il ✓
si elle vient — before elle, no elision ✓
si on y va — before on, no elision ✓

explanation.body: The si clause compresses exactly like any spoken French — si fuses with the pronouns around it but otherwise follows the same reduction rules you already know. The grammar doesn't change; only the sound does.

nodes.title: Relatives II: dont & où

explanation.body: One line to anchor what you know: qui is the subject of its clause, que is the object. Dont and où fill two more slots — de + noun, and place/time.

examples.french: le mec qui parle / le mec que je connais
examples.english: the guy who's talking / the guy I know

explanation.body: Où covers both 'where' and 'when' in relative clauses — French uses the same word for both. English switches to 'when' for time; French doesn't.

examples.french: Le café où on va est sympa.
examples.english: The café we go to is nice.

examples.french: Le jour où je suis arrivé, il faisait beau.
examples.english: The day when I arrived, the weather was nice.

explanation.header: dont: whenever the verb takes de
explanation.body: Dont replaces de + a noun. The verb in the relative clause tells you which pronoun to use: if that verb normally takes de, you need dont — not que, not qui.
bullets: parler de quelqu'un → le mec dont je t'ai parlé (the guy I told you about)
avoir besoin de quelque chose → ce dont j'ai besoin (what I need)
se souvenir de → le film dont je me souviens (the film I remember)

explanation.body: This is the practical rule: look at the verb inside the relative clause and ask what preposition it takes normally. De → dont. No preposition, object → que. Subject → qui. Là → où.

examples.french: C'est le boulot dont j'ai besoin.
examples.english: It's the job I need. (avoir besoin DE)

examples.french: C'est la meuf dont je t'avais parlé.
examples.english: That's the girl I'd told you about. (parler DE)

explanation.body: Unlike ne-drop or on-for-nous, relatives don't simplify in speech — le jour où, la meuf dont j'te parlais come out intact even in fast street French. They show up constantly in storytelling, so recognising them matters as much as producing them.

nodes.title: Verb + preposition patterns: essayer de, commencer à

explanation.body: When two verbs chain in French, only the first verb conjugates — the second stays as an infinitive. Which preposition sits between them depends entirely on the first verb. Learn each verb with its preposition as one unit: vouloir-direct, commencer-à, essayer-de.
bullets: Direct (no preposition): vouloir, pouvoir, aller, aimer, devoir
à-verbs: commencer à, apprendre à, réussir à, arriver à
de-verbs: essayer de, arrêter de, décider de, oublier de, venir de, éviter de

explanation.body: These verbs all express effort toward a result, which is why French marks them with à — a directional preposition. The pattern is verb + à + infinitive; once you hear the à, you know an infinitive follows.

examples.english: Did you manage to do it?

explanation.body: These verbs treat the second action as a separate object — something tried, stopped, forgotten, or avoided — so French uses de, which here marks a detachment. The pattern is verb + de + infinitive.
Before doing something in French, use avant de + infinitive. The second verb never conjugates — it's always the bare infinitive, no matter who the subject is.

In spoken French, à stays as-is, but de elides to d' before a vowel sound — the same rule as any other de in the language. Neither preposition disappears; they just compress. The infinitive after them never changes.

canonical_examples.english: We're trying to find a solution.
canonical_examples.formal: Nous essayons de trouver une solution.
canonical_examples.street: On essaie de trouver une solution.

drills.english: Did you manage to do it?
drills.french_formal: Est-ce que tu as réussi à le faire ?

nodes.title: Two pronouns at once: je te le dis

explanation.body: French lets you replace both the direct and indirect object in one go: instead of 'donne le livre à Marie', you say 'donne-le-lui'. The verb stays in place; the pronouns cluster in front of it in a fixed order.

explanation.header: The order, memorised as a slot chain
explanation.body: There are five slots, left to right. In practice you only ever fill two at once — one from the left side and one from the right.
bullets: slot 1 — me / te / nous / vous
slot 2 — le / la / les
slot 3 — lui / leur
slot 4 — y
slot 5 — en

explanation.header: The pairs that do all the work
explanation.body: You can't combine two from the same slot, and some combinations are simply unnatural. Five pairs cover nearly everything you'll say or hear.
bullets: me + le → me le (il me le donne — he gives it to me)
te + le → te le (je te le dis — I'm telling you)
le + lui → le lui (je le lui explique — I explain it to him)
me + en → m'en (donne-m'en — give me some)
y + en → y en (y en a — there's some)

explanation.body: In a compound tense the pronouns go between the subject and the auxiliary, exactly as with a single pronoun — because the auxiliary is the conjugated verb here. The past participle comes after.

explanation.header: y en a — the most common double
explanation.body: Il y en a (there is/are some) contracts to y'en a in speech. It's the single most frequent double-pronoun form in French, so learn it as a chunk before anything else.

In fast speech the pronouns fuse into one continuous sound block before the verb — you stop hearing gaps between them. That block behaves as a unit: catch any part of it and you know where the verb is.

canonical_examples.english: There's none left, I can tell you that.
canonical_examples.formal: Il y en a plus, je peux te le dire.
canonical_examples.street: Y'en a plus, j'peux te l'dire.

nodes.title: Degree words: trop, grave, carrément

explanation.body: Four words scale adjectives in standard French, and they all sit directly before the adjective — the position never moves. Think of them as a volume knob going from quiet to loud.
bullets: un peu — a little (un peu fatigué)
assez — fairly (assez bien)
très — very (très bien)
vraiment — really (vraiment bien)

explanation.body: In formal French, trop means 'too much' — trop chaud means dangerously hot. In speech, under-40 French uses trop exactly where textbooks say très. The literal meaning fades; the intensity stays. You will hear almost no très in casual conversation.

explanation.header: The street ladder: grave, carrément, vachement, hyper
explanation.body: These four words are the real spoken scale — they replace très and vraiment for anyone who wants to sound like they actually live in France. They all follow the same rule: degree word directly before the adjective.
bullets: grave — c'est grave bien (that's seriously good)
carrément — c'est carrément nul (that's completely rubbish)
vachement — c'est vachement bon (it's really good)
hyper / super — j'suis hyper content, c'est super cool

explanation.header: grave and carrément as full answers
explanation.body: Both words double as one-word reactions to a yes/no question. There is no adjective in play — the word carries the full force of 'absolutely' or 'totally' on its own. You already saw these in the chunks_reactions lesson; now you know why they work: they are just degree words turned up all the way.

In French, a degree word scales exactly what follows it — put it anywhere else and the sentence breaks. This holds for every item on both ladders: un peu, très, trop, grave, carrément all land immediately before the adjective or adverb they modify. There is no exception to hunt for.

nodes.title: Discourse glue: du coup, donc, alors, bref

explanation.body: Du coup means 'so' or 'as a result' and is the most common spoken connector in French. It chains any two thoughts — cause and effect, change of plan, simple next step — without needing a logical relationship as tight as English 'therefore'. If you use it roughly every four sentences, you already sound more fluent.

examples.french: J'avais faim, du coup j'ai mangé.
examples.english: I was hungry, so I ate.

examples.french: T'as pas répondu, du coup j'suis parti.
examples.english: You didn't answer, so I left.

explanation.header: Donc vs alors: same word, different job
explanation.body: Donc closes logic — it draws a conclusion. Alors moves a story forward or, when stretched as alors…, buys thinking time while you find your next word. They often look interchangeable but the rhythm is different: donc lands; alors floats.

examples.french: T'as rien mangé, donc t'as faim — c'est normal.
examples.english: You ate nothing, so you're hungry — makes sense.

examples.french: Alors… j'sais pas, chais pas quoi dire.
examples.english: So… I don't know, I don't know what to say.

explanation.header: Passke, mais, par contre: cause and contrast
explanation.body: Parce que — 'because' — collapses to passke in real speech; spelling doesn't change, pronunciation does. Mais is a blunt contrast. Par contre ('on the other hand') is softer and extremely common in spoken French for qualifying what you just said.
bullets: passke = parce que — 'J'peux pas venir passke j'dois bosser.'
mais — direct opposition: 'J'veux venir, mais j'peux pas.'
par contre — soft qualifier: 'C'est bien, par contre c'est chaud.'

explanation.header: Sequencing a story: d'abord to bref
explanation.body: D'abord (first), après or ensuite (then/next), and bref (long story short) are the scaffold of any spoken anecdote. Bref is especially useful: it signals you're cutting the rest and landing the point, which is exactly what fast, confident speakers do.

examples.french: D'abord on est allés au resto, après y'avait plus de place, du coup on a mangé chez moi — bref, c'était bien.
examples.english: First we went to the restaurant, then there were no tables left, so we ate at mine — anyway, it was good.

explanation.body: En fait means 'actually' and almost always opens a correction or a reframe of what was just said or assumed. It doesn't mean 'in fact' in the formal sense — it signals 'wait, here's the real picture'. Use it at the start of a sentence.

examples.french: En fait, c'est pas si grave.
En fait j'voulais pas y aller.
examples.english: Actually I didn't want to go.

explanation.body: These words are what fills the gaps between sentences in real speech. A hesitation that sounds fluent in French is alors… or du coup… not silence. Passke replaces parce que, bref cuts any story short — using them correctly signals you are processing in French, not translating.
bullets: du coup — every 4 sentences, any register
passke — always in speech; parce que only in writing
bref — ends any story; can stand alone as a one-word summary
alors… (stretched) — thinking pause, same job as 'um' but sounds intentional
